content:
1. Introduction

Adult mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), isolated and derived from connective tissue sources, have considerable translational potential for cell-based therapies using autologous transplantation due to their plasticity, accessibility, multipotent nature and limited ethical concerns [1–5]. However, recent studies have shown that the therapeutic effect of MSCs depends on their paracrine activity or secretion of bioactive molecules, such as exosomes, rather than the engraftment and differentiation ability at the injury or disease site [6–11]. Therefore, over the past decade, the emphasis has shifted toward harnessing the MSC's ability to produce exosomes as novel therapeutics tools to prevent disease initiation/progression [12–15]. Moreover, the MSC-derived exosomes have been investigated in clinical trials for therapeutic applications, highlighting their clinical relevance (ClinicalTrials.gov ID NCT05216562 and NCT05787288). Exosomes are nano-sized (30-150 nm) membrane-bound vesicles secreted by the cells modulating cellular signaling by transforming crucial information in the form of lipids, proteins, mRNAs and small RNAs (i.e., miRNAs) to maintain physiological homeostasis [12–15].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
The use of MSCs produced exosomes, in lieu of MSCs transplantation, has certain advantages; 1) higher safety profile, 2) lower immunogenicity, 3) the ability to cross biological barriers, and 4) avoiding complications that arise from cell transplantation, such as immune reactions or rejection, infections and hematologic complications [12–21]. However, the most important challenges in using exosomes as potential therapeutic tools are to 1) achieve enough amount with mass production allowing clinically relevant treatment tools, 2) obtain products with high bioactivity necessary for the therapeutic action, 3) isolation with high yield and purity [12–22]. Although each of these challenges are crucial to be addressed, this study specifically tackles the challenge of isolating bioactive exosomes from MSCs culture media with high yield and purity by developing a stimuli responsive polymer-based ultrafiltration membrane.

The current strategies for exosome isolation typically involve a combination of two or more sequential steps, most commonly ultracentrifugation, ultrafiltration, size exclusion chromatography (SEC), and precipitation [23–26]. Among these, ultracentrifugation has long been regarded as the "gold standard" technique for exosome isolation due to its ability to separate vesicles based on density and size with relatively high reproducibility [27–30].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: However, despite its widespread use in research, ultracentrifugation is not directly scalable for clinical manufacturing. The method requires specialized equipment, is time-consuming, and often results in limited yield, making it unsuitable for large-scale production where clinical translation demands both efficiency and cost-effectiveness [23–26]. Recent experimental studies have highlighted filtration and ultrafiltration as promising alternatives for large-scale exosome production [23–26,29,31–36]. These approaches are simpler, more adaptable to industrial workflows, and can be integrated into continuous manufacturing pipelines to produce high mass of exosomes while preserving biological activity. Evidence from comparative studies further supports the advantages of ultrafiltration [29,31–35,35,36]. While ultrafiltration enables relatively higher exosome yield, the exosome purity in general is limited because of lower rejection rate of free proteins or biomolecules in cell culture media resulting in decreased purity. In addition, possible biofouling on the membrane surface may result in decrease in yield and purity over time [23,25,31,35–38].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: Alternatively, combining ultrafiltration with complementary methods such as SEC can significantly improve purity, thereby ensuring that the isolated vesicles are free from protein aggregates and other biomolecules or contaminants [31,33,34]. Although this hybrid approach improves yield and purity together, it requires multiple processing steps. Taken together, the literature reports underscore the urgent need for an alternative isolation strategy that can deliver exosomes with high yield and purity. Therefore, in this study, we developed a temperature and pH responsive pentablock co-polymer (PBC)-based ultrafiltration membrane to isolate bioactive exosomes with high yield and purity directly from MSCs culture media in single step. We specifically selected temperature and pH responsive PBC for this study because MSCs cell culture operates at 37°C and at pH 7.4. Moreover, most of the exosome isolation methods are performed at 4°C and at pH 7.4 to keep biological activity and stability of the exosomes during the isolation process [37–41]. Our PBC demonstrated temperature (at 4 and 37°C) and pH (at 7.4) responsive conformational changes controlling the surface behavior of the membrane, which in turn modulated permeability, protein/biomolecule rejection and potential antibiofouling properties enhancing the yield and purity [42–45].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
In addition, since the PBC can operate at 37°C and at pH 7.4 by changing conformation, the PBC-based membrane could be possibly integrated into a bioreactor system to enable continuous exosome isolation. Although the PBC is responsive to various pH values below and above its critical pH value (pH 7.6) [42–45], the effect of pH was not investigated in detail as the pH of the cell culture media is set to 7.4 to enable optimum cellular growth and viability.

The membrane was developed by modifying the surface of sulfonated polysulfone support with subsequent polyelectrolyte layers and stimuli responsive PBC via electrostatic interactions. The membrane was characterized in terms of structure, hydrophilicity and permeability. The size cutoff of the membrane was determined using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with size range of 50-200 nm mimicking the actual exosome size range. The isolation of exosomes directly from the MSCs culture media was performed and the protein rejection, exosome yield and purity were determined via filtration test and compared with commercially available exosome isolation kit. The obtained exosomes were characterized in terms of size, shape and exosome integrity as well as bioactivity.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Our results demonstrated that the developed membrane showed better or at least comparable bioactive exosome yield and purity performance as compared to commercial kits and other studies in the literature emphasizing its potential for future development or integration into continuous exosome bioproduction systems.

2. Experimental

2.1. Materials

Polysulfone (PSF) (Mw: 35 kDa), alginic acid sodium salt from brown algae (ALG) (80-120 kDa) and polyethyleneimine (PEI; Mw: 25 kDa), trimethylsilyl chlorosulfonate (TMSCS), sodium methoxide 15% in methanol solution, and chloroform were purchased from Sigma. Solvents, 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc) with a purity of >99.5 and >99% respectively, were purchased from Sigma. Methylene blue and Congo red solutions were supplied from Sigma. Gold nanoparticles with three different sizes (50, 100, and 200 nm) from NanoPartz.

2.2. Methods

2.2.1. Synthesis of stimuli responsive pentablock co-polymers (PBC)

The temperature and pH responsive cationic and amphiphilic pentablock co-polymers (PBC), which consist of the temperature responsive middle block Pluronic F127 (triblock co-polymer formed by polyethylene oxide(PEO)-polypropylene oxide(PPO)-polyethylene oxide (PEO) or PEO-PPO-PEO) and pH responsive cationic PDEAEM (poly(2-diethylaminoethyl methacrylate)) end blocks (PDEAEM–Pluronic F127–PDEAEM), were synthesized by atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) and characterized by 1H NMR using a Varian MR-400 (Varian Inc., Palo Alto, CA) as explained elsewhere [42–47].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
2.2.2. Synthesis of sulfonated polysulfone (SPSF)

Sulfonated polysulfone (SPSF) was synthesized as described previously [48–50]. Briefly, 20 g of polysulfone (PSF) was mixed with 300 mL of chloroform and stirred in a three-neck round bottom flask for 24 h. Then, 25 mL of trimethylchlorosilane was added dropwise to the solution for 10 min under continuous nitrogen purge. Following 24 h of reaction, 12.5 mL of sodium methoxide solution (15% in methanol) was added to the solution and mixed for 1 h. After an hour, the solution was poured into methanol to precipitate the sulfonated polysulfone (SPSF). The SPSF was collected and washed with deionized (DI) water under vacuum to remove impurities. The washed SPSF was then dried in a vacuum oven for 24 h at 80°C to remove any remaining solvent and water. The sulfonation of PSF was confirmed by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis.

2.2.3. Development of PBC membrane

The development of PBC membrane was adopted and modified based on our previous work [42]. Briefly, 25 wt% of PSF and SPSF (with 3:1 w/w ratio) was dissolved in a mixture of NMP and DMAc (with 1:2 v/v ratio) for 24 h forming the casting solution.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Then, the solution was cast on a glass substrate using an automatic film applicator (TQC Sheen) using 250 μm casting knife and the membrane was immediately immersed into 0.5 wt% polyethyleneimine (PEI) solution for 24 h. After washing the excess PEI with DI water, the membrane was subsequently coated with 1 mg/mL of alginate (ALG) solution at pH 4 using dead-end filtration cell (Millipore Sigma Amicon) at 1 bar for 15 min. The membrane was washed with DI water for 5 min to remove excess ALG. At the final step, the membrane was coated with PBC solution at pH 4 for 150 min by filtering 18 mg/mL of PBC using the dead-end filtration cell at 2 bar.

2.2.4. Characterization of PBC membrane

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PHI VersaProbe 5000 X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy) was used to confirm the changes in the surface chemistry and successful coating after each layer modification. A monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) with X-ray size of 200 μm was used in XPS analysis with an electron take-off angle of 45° from a normal sampling surface. Survey scans were collected from 10 to 1100 eV with a pass energy of 187.85 eV.

The successful layer-by-layer modification was demonstrated by methylene blue (positively charged) and Congo red (negatively charged) staining via visual observation of the change in the surface charge after each layer modification.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
The layer-by-layer modification was also indirectly proved by pure water permeability measurement in dead-end filtration system (Amicon). The 50 mL of DI water filtration was performed after each layer modification on a membrane with an active surface area of 7.25 cm² at 2 bar at ambient temperature. Compaction at 2.5 bar was applied before each filtration. Equations (1) and (2) were used to determine the water flux and the permeability: Flux = ΔV/(A * Δt) (mL/m²·min); Permeability = ΔV/(A * Δt * ΔP) (mL/m²·min·bar).

The pore size distribution of the membranes after each layer modification was evaluated by combining experimentally measured solute rejection data with Equations (3)-(8) as described in our previous study [42]. Thiol modified polyethylene glycol (SH-PEG – MW 1000 Da) coated gold nanoparticles (AuNPs – size: 50, 100 and 200 nm), prepared through gold-sulfur interactions and characterized as described in our previous work [47,51], were used as neutral model solutes to obtain rejection data for each layer modification. The mean effective pore radius (μp) and pore size distribution (σp) were determined by fitting the experimental AuNP-PEG rejection data to probability density function model using Equation (9). It was assumed that there is no steric and hydrodynamic interaction between the neutral AuNP-PEG and pores of the membranes [42].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
The changes in theoretically calculated pore size distribution after each layer modification were also confirmed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM-ThermoFisher Apreo 2S) cross sectional image analysis. Briefly, membrane samples after each layer modification were cut using liquid nitrogen for cross-section, mounted on vertical stubs, sputter-coated with platinum, and cross-section images were taken using secondary electron mode. ImageJ software was used for image analysis to determine the changes in membrane thickness and average pore size in the dense skin layer.

2.2.5. Determination of PBC membrane size cutoff

Negatively charged gold nanoparticles (bare AuNPs, not coated with PEG) with the size of 50, 100 and 200 nm in diameter were used as model particles mimicking the surface charge and size range (30-150 nm) of exosomes, which are also negatively charged due to exosomal membrane components. The calibration curve of "Absorbance versus AuNPs concentration" for each AuNP size was constructed using known concentrations of AuNPs and corresponding absorbance values measured in UV-spectrophotometer (BioTek Synergy H1 Plate Reader) at size specific wavelengths of 558, 570, and 532 for 200, 100, and 50 nm AuNPs, respectively.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
The size-based rejection of AuNPs from the membranes at different operating temperatures and pH values was determined using dead-end filter unit. Briefly, the AuNP suspensions with different particle sizes were pre-adjusted in terms of temperature (4 and 37°C) and pH (pH 4 and 7.4) before the filtration. The filtration was performed in temperature-controlled environment as described above and the AuNP concentrations in feed and permeate were determined using absorbance measurements and constructed calibration curves. Then, the percent rejection of AuNPs for each operating condition was calculated using Equation (10): R(%) = (1 - C_p/C_feed) * 100. Following the filtration, the membranes were removed from the filtration cell and the AuNPs accumulated on the membrane surface were imaged by SEM.

2.2.6. MSCs culture and isolation of exosomes using PBC membrane

Bone marrow derived rat-MSCs were cultured in T75 flasks (with initial seeding density of 2 x 10^6 cells) until 80% confluency by using our previously established cell culturing procedures [52–56]. Once they reached 80% confluency, the media was replaced by exosome depleted phenol free (clear in color and transparent) media. These media modifications were specifically selected; 1) to eliminate exosomes coming from the serum and ensure the isolated exosomes are only from the secretome of cultured MSCs, and 2) to eliminate any potential interference during the measurements that may result from the phenolic pink color of the cell culture media.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Following the media change, the cells were continued for incubation for two more days, and the cell culture media (15 mL) was collected for exosome isolation. The collected culture media was directly filtered through the membrane without any pre-processing (such as centrifugation of culture media to remove cell debris before filtration) at selected operation temperatures (4 and 37°C at pH 7.4) using dead-end filter unit as described above. Before the filtration test, the cell culture media temperature was kept same (for 37°C) or pre-adjusted (for 4°C) and the filtration set up was maintained in an incubator with constant temperature during the filtration. The cell culture media permeability was determined using Equations (1) and (2) after collecting the volume passing through the membrane per time data as described above. In addition, the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of the membrane surface was also assessed by contact angle analysis. A 10 μL droplet of cell culture media (free from cells and pre-adjusted at different temperatures (4 and 37°C at pH 7.4) was deposited onto the samples in an environmental chamber and contact angle measurements were recorded.

After the filtration test, the concentration of free proteins coming from the culture media in the feed and permeate was determined using Qubit Protein Assay Kit (Thermo) and the percent rejection was calculated using Equation (10).

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
The total exosome concentration (or amount) in the permeate was determined using EXOCET-Exosome Quantitation Kit (System Biosciences). It was noted that although exosome concentration is represented as "number of exosomes/mL" in ExoCET kit, to avoid confusion and align with field-wide standards, "particles/mL" was used as unit for exosome quantitation throughout the text. To calculate the purity of the isolated exosomes, the exosome concentration values obtained with EXOCET kit in the permeate were divided by the concentration of free protein obtained by the Qubit Protein Assay kit in the permeate. This gives the purity as total number of exosomes (or particles) per microgram free protein in the permeate.

In addition to the total exosome concentration, we also evaluated the total exosomal protein concentration in the permeate. For this purpose, the isolated exosomes in the permeate were lysed using RIPA buffer to break the exosomal membrane and leach out the exosomal protein content as described before [57,58]. Then, BCA protein assay (Thermo Pierce) was performed to assess the total exosomal protein concentration in the permeate. The Qubit Protein Assay was utilized for permeate samples because of its high sensitivity in fluorometric detection and its resistance to interference from reducing sugars, such as glucose, which are abundant in cell culture media and can disrupt BCA assay chemistry [59–61]. In contrast, quantification of exosomal proteins after RIPA lysis necessitated the BCA assay, since the high detergent concentration in RIPA is incompatible with the Qubit assay but is tolerated by the BCA assay [59–61].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
To evaluate and compare the exosome isolation efficiency of the PBC membranes, the commercially available exosome isolation kit, ExoQuick-TC (System Biosciences), was used as positive control to isolate the exosomes. The exosome isolation efficiency of the PBC membranes were also additionally compared with established exosome isolation methods, such as ultracentrifugation (UC) and size exclusion chromatography (SEC). For UC based isolation, previously established methods involving subsequent centrifugation steps (13,000g for 30 min; 210,000g for 30 min at 4°C; and 3100,000g for 70 min at 4°C) were followed as described before [28]. For SEC based isolation, qEV Original Gen2 70 nm (IZON #ICO-70) was used. Note that for all isolation methods, we used the same initial cell seeding density (2 x 10^6 cells) and same volume of collected cell culture media.

2.2.7. Membrane reusability

The membrane reusability and performance stability over multiple filtration cycles were also tested for the membrane operated at 37°C and pH 7.4. The membranes were subjected to five subsequent cell culture media filtration cycles using identical operating conditions. After the filtration cycles were completed, the membrane was backwashed with DI water to remove loosely adsorbed media constituents (mainly high molecular weight proteins, protein complexes, lipoproteins, and other macromolecular aggregates) and retested. Performance retention was quantified by assessing cell culture media permeability, protein rejection, exosome yield, purity and exosomal protein amount. The membrane fouling after filtration cycles and back-wash was also evaluated using SEM imaging.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
2.2.8. Characterization of isolated exosomes

Exosome size, distribution and concentration were determined using nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA-Particle Metrix ZetaView). The size and shape of the isolated exosomes were also determined by Transmission Scanning Electron Microscopy (TSEM). For TSEM imaging, the exosomes were fixed by 2% paraformaldehyde and after washing, the suspension was dropped onto formvar/carbon coated copper grids with 200 mesh for drying. The images were taken in ThermoFisher Apreo 2S using TSEM detector.

In addition, exosomal proteins and membrane of the isolated exosomes in the permeate were labeled using ExoGlow-Protein EV Labeling Kit and ExoGlow-Membrane EV Labeling Kit (System Biosciences). We also checked common exosome markers (such as CD9, CD63 and CD81) using marker specific Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assays (ExoELISA-ULTRA Complete Kit for CD9, CD63 and CD81 from System Biosciences).

To confirm that the isolated exosomes are MSCs-derived, Western blot analysis was also performed as described before [56]. Briefly, isolated exosomes were lysed in RIPA buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.4, 150 mM NaCl, 0.25% deoxycholic acid, 1% NP-40, 1 mM EDTA) supplemented with protease and phosphatase inhibitors. Lysates were incubated on ice, briefly sonicated, and centrifuged at 20,000g for 15 min at 4°C. Protein concentrations were determined using the BCA assay.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Equal protein amounts (10 μg) were separated on 4–20% SDS-PAGE gels and transferred to PVDF membranes using a semi-dry transfer system (Invitrogen). Membranes were blocked in 1x Clear Milk Blocking Buffer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) for 1 h at room temperature and incubated overnight at 4°C with primary antibodies (1:1000). The MSCs markers, CD29 (Invitrogen, MA5-31981), CD44 (Invitrogen, MA5-53338), and CD73 (Invitrogen, 12231-1-AP), were selected for the analysis. After washing with TBS-T, membranes were incubated with HRP-conjugated goat-anti-rabbit secondary antibody (1:50,000, Invitrogen, 31460) for 45 min, washed, and developed using enhanced chemiluminescence. Bands were imaged using an Azure300 system.

2.2.9. Biological activity of the isolated exosomes

The biological activity of the isolated exosomes in the permeate was evaluated using two different bioactivity tests: 1) a scratch test on 3T3 fibroblast cells to assess the effect of exosomes on cell migration, and 2) a neurite outgrowth test on PC12-TrkB cells to assess the effect of exosomes on potential neuronal regeneration.

For the scratch test, 3T3 fibroblast cells (ATCC CRL-1658) were cultured as instructed by the manufacturer. The cells were seeded on 6 well plates (initial seeding density 5 x 10^5 cell per well) and grown until full confluency. Once fully confluent, the cells in the middle of each well were scratched vertically from top to bottom using 1 mL sterile pipette tip to create a continuous gap.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Then, the cell culture media was replaced to remove the scratched cells and 1 x 10^10 exosomes were added into 2 mL cell culture media. The scratched cells without exosome treatment were used as control. After 24 h of incubation, at least 10 pictures were taken throughout the vertical scratch from each well with same magnification, the gap closure was measured using ImageJ software, and percent gap closure was calculated with respect to control.

For the neurite outgrowth test, PC12-TrkB cells (ATCC CRL-1721) were cultured as instructed by the manufacturer. The cells were seeded on laminin coated 6 well plates (initial seeding density 3 x 10^5 cell per well) and incubated overnight for cellular attachment and growth. The following day, 1 x 10^10 exosomes were added into 2 mL cell culture media and further incubated for another 48 h. The non-exosome treated cells were used as control. At the end of incubation, the cells were fixed and immunocytochemical (ICC) staining was performed using neuronal marker β–III–tubulin (Tuj1) as described before [53,56,62]. Briefly, after the incubation, the cells were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde, blocked, and incubated with primary antibody β–III–tubulin (Tuj1) (1:200, Proteintech, 66240-1-Ig). The following day, samples were labeled with goat-anti-mouse-AF488 (1:400, Invitrogen, A-21131) and DAPI (4',6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) (1:500, Thermo Fisher Scientific, 62248) for nuclear staining. After 90 min of incubation in the dark at room temperature, samples were rinsed with PBS and imaged by fluorescence microscopy (Nikon Eclipse Ts2R-FL).

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
At least 10 images per sample were taken, and the total length of the neurites, along with the total number of cells, were analyzed using ImageJ software.

2.2.10. Statistical analysis

Statistical significance was evaluated using one-way ANOVA analysis by Tukey's method with a 95% confidence interval. At least three independent experiments were conducted for each analysis. Mean ± standard deviation is used to present the results.

3. Results and discussion

Pentablock copolymer (PBC) structure: The stimuli responsive PBC was successfully synthesized and characterized in terms of molecular weight (MW: ~15 kDa as per the 1H NMR analysis (Supporting Information Figure S1)) and similar results were found as in our previous publications [42,46,47]. The chemical structure of the PBC (PDEAEM13-b-PEO100-b-PPO65-b-PEO100-b-PDEAEM13) is shown in (Supporting Information Figure S2). The central poly(ethylene oxide)–block–poly(propylene oxide)–block–poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO–PPO–PEO or Pluronic F127) middle block imparts temperature-responsive behavior to the polymer, while the terminal cationic poly(2-diethylaminoethyl methacrylate) (PDEAEM) end blocks contribute to the pH-responsive nature of the PBC.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
In atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) reaction, the hydroxyl end groups of Pluronic F127 are modified with α-bromoisobutyrate to create a macroinitiator. In the presence of a Cu(I)/ligand catalyst, brominated ends of the macroinitiator undergo reversible atom transfer to initiate polymerization of DEAEM monomer from symmetric ends of the Pluronic F127 core. Continuous activation–deactivation between Cu(I) and Cu(II) allows controlled chain growth. As a result, PDEAEM blocks grow symmetrically from each terminus of the F127 backbone, yielding the well-defined PDEAEM–Pluronic F127–PDEAEM pentablock copolymer [43–45,47].

Sulfonated polysulfone (SPSF) structure: The chemical structure of sulfonated polysulfone (SPSF), forming the base membrane, was confirmed by FTIR (Supporting Information, Fig. S3) showing similar peaks in the spectra as reported by other studies in the literature [48–50]. The peaks at FTIR spectra demonstrated the stretching of S–O and S=O groups (broad band (1000-1200 cm⁻¹) at around 1100 and 1170 cm⁻¹) due to the introduction of hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups (-SO3H) onto the aromatic polysulfone (PSF) backbone via electrophilic substitution. In addition, the band at around 600 and 620 cm⁻¹ represented S–O stretching in the sulfonate group, and the band at around 790 cm⁻¹ belonged to C–H bond in PSF [48].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: Membrane modification: The SPSF/PSF support membrane modification was primarily governed by subsequent layer-by-layer coating of different polyelectrolytes through electrostatic interactions. Introduction of sulfonated groups to the PSF base membrane created negatively charged support membrane that allows coating of positively charged polyethyleneimine (PEI) layer through electrostatic interactions. PEI contains primary, secondary, and tertiary amine groups that can be protonated at near-neutral or acidic solutions and become positively charged (or cationic) [63]. This resulted in obtaining the SPSF/PSF-PEI membrane with a positively charged surface, enabling the subsequent electrostatic coating of the negatively charged alginate (ALG) layer. Near-neutral to acidic conditions, the carboxyl groups (-COOH) of ALG are deprotonated to carboxylate anions (COO-), creating a negatively charged polymer that electrostatically interact with cations [64]. Following SPSF/PSF-PEI-ALG membrane formation, the last layer of cationic PBC was coated through electrostatic interactions. At pH 4, the PBC forms protonated tertiary amine groups existing in PDEAEM end blocks, enabling electrostatic coating on the ALG layer.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: Characterization of membrane modification: The successful layer-by-layer coating and surface chemistry of each layer was demonstrated by XPS analysis (Fig. 1). It was observed that the sulfur content of the SPSF/PSF support membrane (S2p content) continuously decreased and eventually disappeared upon consecutive PEI, ALG and PBC modification in the survey scans (Fig. 1A). In addition, this trend could also be supported by the appearance and disappearance of sodium (Na 1s content) in the survey scans upon subsequent ALG and PBC modification (Fig. 1A). Similarly, the changes in the C–C/C–H, C–O/C–N and C=O in high resolution C1 spectra also demonstrated the associated chemical changes upon each layer modification based on the coated polyelectrolyte structure (Fig. 1B). It was observed that the C=O peak at ~288 eV was very low after PEI coating as there was no C=O bonding in PEI structure. However, the amount of C=O bond significantly increased after ALG coating as its structure contains C=O bonds. The C=O content showed reduction after PBC coating due to the effect of coating but did not completely disappear as PBC also contains C=O bond in PDEAEM segment (Fig. 1B).

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Similarly, C–C/C–H bond content at ~285 eV was significantly high after PEI coating, which decreased and stayed stable after subsequent ALG and PBC coating as both layer components have similar C–C/C–H content (Fig. 1B). As another observation, C–O/C–N content at ~286 eV was similar after PEI and ALG layers but significantly increased after PBC coating (Fig. 1B). All these observations can be attributed to the changes of surface chemistry after successful modification of each layer.

The layer-by-layer modification of SPSF/PSF support membrane was visually observed by methylene blue (positively charged) and Congo red (negatively charged) staining upon each polyelectrolyte layer modification (Supporting Information, Fig. S5). This visually and qualitatively demonstrates how the surface charge changes after each layer modification.

The successful layer-by-layer modification was also indirectly confirmed by pure water permeability results at room temperature. As can be seen in Fig. 2A, as the PEI, ALG and PBC layers were subsequently deposited on the SPSF/PSF support, the pure water permeability decreased significantly from ~300 to ~20 mL/(m²·min·bar) as expected. This also correlates with the theoretically calculated pore size distribution of the membranes after each layer modification.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: The theoretical calculations based on the experimental rejection data combined with probability density function demonstrated that the mean pore size was ~400 nm for SPSF/PSF-PEI, ~75 nm for SPSF/PSF-PEI-ALG and ~113 nm for SPSF/PSF-PEI-ALG-PBC (Fig. 2B). This was further supported by mean pore size obtained from the dense skin layer of cross-sectional SEM images (Fig. 2C). The mean pore size of the dense skin layer obtained from SEM images were ~800 nm for SPSF/PSF-PEI, ~118 nm for SPSF/PSF-PEI-ALG and ~95 nm for SPSF/PSF-PEI-ALG-PBC. Although there is certain deviation between the theoretical calculation and SEM image analysis, the decrease of mean pore size upon each layer modification follows the same trend. In addition, the final mean pore size after PBC layer coating (~95 ± 27 nm - 111 ± 2.9 nm) was within the range of exosome size (30-150 nm). The overall thickness of the membrane also changed after each layer modification. Based on the cross-sectional SEM images, it was observed that the overall thickness of the membrane was ~139 μm for SPSF/PSF-PEI, ~202 μm for SPSF/PSF-PEI-ALG and ~213 μm for SPSF/PSF-PEI-ALG-PBC (Fig. 2D). The mean thickness of the dense skin layer after the PBC modification was also measured as 6 ± 1.4 μm.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: Determination of size cutoff: The size cutoff of the PBC membrane was determined by assessing the percent rejection of AuNPs at different sizes (50, 100 and 200 nm, mimicking the size range of exosomes) under different operating temperatures (4 and 37°C) and pH values (pH 4 and 7.4). The percent rejection for 50 and 100 nm AuNPs were found to be significantly lower (~18% and ~48%, respectively) while the rejection of 200 nm AuNPs was determined as ~80% under different operating conditions (Fig. 3A). The rejection performance of PBC membrane for 200 nm AuNPs did not change significantly in response to temperature and/or pH (Fig. 3A). The stable rejection of PBC membrane could be due to the PBC layer changing the surface properties (i.e., hydrophilicity or conformation) rather than altering the effective pore size relative to the AuNP size. Therefore, the AuNP rejection could be mostly governed by steric exclusion of the base membrane, which remains constant under these conditions, and the role of PBC layer potentially modulated antifouling and tunable surface interactions, rather than shifting the rejection cutoff. Similar trends were also reported in previous studies demonstrating the membrane separation performance remained constant under different stimuli responsive environment [42,65–67].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
The SEM images in Fig. 3B show the accumulated 200 nm AuNPs on the PBC membrane surface after filtration. The visual amount of filtered AuNPs in feed, permeate and retentate as well as on the surface of the actual PBC membranes can also be seen in Supporting Information, Fig. S6.

The pH and temperature responsiveness of PBC membrane: The effect of pH was not specifically investigated in this work, as the main objective of this study is to isolate exosomes directly from cell culture media at pH 7.4 and at 37°C. However, while pH exerts only a minor influence on the rejection of 200 nm gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) by the PBC membrane at 37°C (having slightly higher rejection at pH 7.4) (Fig. 3A), consistent with steric exclusion as the dominant transport mechanism, the same pH shift produces a pronounced effect on protein rejection during filtration of cell culture media (Supporting Information, Fig. S7). This difference can be ascribed to the rigid, non-deformable nature of AuNPs, whose passage is mainly determined by size, with limited sensitivity to variations in membrane charge or hydration. In contrast, proteins in cell culture media display pH-dependent alterations in charge, conformation, aggregation state, and membrane affinity. Concurrently, the PDEAEM-containing membrane undergoes protonation-induced changes in surface charge and hydrophilicity, which strongly influence protein-surface interactions.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Consequently, protein rejection is responsive to pH-dependent interfacial interactions, whereas AuNP rejection remains largely unaffected and driven by steric exclusion. When the temperature was kept constant (either at 4°C or 37°C), it was observed that the PBC membrane operated at pH 7.4 provided significantly higher rejection rate compared to the membrane operated at pH 4. Although this demonstrated the pH responsiveness of the PBC layer for the protein rejection from the cell culture media, rejection values obtained at different temperatures and pH conditions were below ~50%, except the membrane operated at 37°C and pH 7.4 (Supporting Information, Fig. S7). Therefore, considering these findings along with the fixed pH of the cell culture media (pH 7.4), the pH responsiveness of this membrane was not further investigated.

The temperature responsiveness of the top PBC layer was also evaluated through the filtration of cell culture media from the membrane at 4 and 37°C at pH 7.4. It was observed that the cell culture media permeability at 37°C was ~3 times higher than the permeability at 4°C (Fig. 4A). This observation was attributed to the potential conformational change of the PBC layer at different temperatures at pH 7.4.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: When the pH is set to 7.4, the PDEAEM block is partially protonated and demonstrates mixed hydrophilic/hydrophobic character as this pH value is very close to its critical pH value (pH 7.6) at which the pendant amines become uncharged. When the temperature is at 4°C, which is below the lower critical solution temperature (LCST: ~20°C), the PBC chains are swollen and become hydrated to form stretched/brush conformations (Pluronic F127 hydrophilic, PDEAEM partially protonated) [42]. On the other hand, when the temperature is at 37°C, which is above the LCST, the PPO block of Pluronic F127 becomes hydrophobic, and PDEAEM partially deprotonates, causing the PBC layer to collapse into a dense, compact layer on the membrane surface [42]. Therefore, a thinner layer formed at 37°C, resulting in higher permeability [42]. The decrease in the contact angle value at 37°C in cell culture media showed enhanced hydrophilic behavior supporting the cell culture media permeability (Fig. 4B). Normally, it is expected to observe relatively more hydrophilic behavior for the PBC membrane at 4°C, where the polymer remains more hydrated, compared to at 37°C, where the polymer collapses and becomes more hydrophobic, when a buffer is used [43,44]. However, the components of the cell culture media (i.e., serum proteins) could possibly influence this transition.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
It is anticipated that the presence of cell culture media constituents and serum proteins mask or change temperature responsiveness of the PBC layer leading to pronounced hydrophilicity at 37°C [68–71]. In addition, the lower viscosity of the cell culture media at 37°C as compared to the viscosity at 4°C also contributed to the higher cell culture media permeability in Fig. 4A [72,73].

There is a general trade-off between the permeability and selectivity meaning that as the permeability increases the rejection performance of a synthetic membrane decrease. Therefore, the efforts in the literature mostly focused on increasing the permeability without compromising the rejection performance [74,75]. However, our results demonstrated that the PBC membrane was able to achieve ~3 times higher cell culture media permeability accompanied by ~2 times higher protein rejection when operated at 37°C at pH 7.4 as compared to when operated at 4°C at pH 7.4 (Fig. 4A and C). This can be explained by the conformational change of the PBC layer. The swollen, hydrated and extended brush-like conformation of PBC layer at 4°C allows partial protein penetration and adsorption, leading to lower protein rejection [42]. On the other hand, the collapsed dense compact conformation of PBC layer at 37°C creates a strong steric and hydration barrier, significantly reducing protein adsorption and penetration, resulting in higher protein rejection [42].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
These conformational changes also resulted in reduced fouling on the membrane surface as seen in Fig. 4D and E. As a comparison and control, the surface of the membrane after the filtration of the cell culture media alone (without cells/exosomes) was also analyzed via SEM imaging. It was observed that filtration of cell culture media alone also did not create significant residues or fouling on the membrane surface (Supporting Information, Fig. S8). Our observations are consistent with previous reports demonstrating reversible conformational changes and tunable separation performance under physiological conditions [42–44,69,76–80]. In general, the temperature-induced collapse of hydrophobic domains and hydration of PEO chains modulate surface wettability, fouling resistance, and transport properties. Therefore, the role of PBC layer is mainly to reduce nonspecific protein fouling rather than controlling the surface hydrophilicity while maintaining high permeability under physiological conditions in cell culture media.

Isolation performance of the membrane: The exosome isolation performance of the PBC membrane was also evaluated at 4 and 37°C and compared with the commercially available exosome isolation kit, ExoQuick-TC (Fig. 5). ExoQuick-TC was selected for comparison considering its competitive performance with respect to other available isolation kits and standard ultracentrifugation.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
It was observed that the total exosome yield and associated total exosomal protein amount in the permeate were similar when PBC membrane operated at 4 or 37°C (Fig. 5A and B). This also indicated that the isolation of the exosomes mostly relied on the steric exclusion of the membrane based on the effective pore size rather than the stimuli responsive behavior of the PBC layer mainly modulating the surface properties. Moreover, it was also noted that the total exosome yield and associated total exosomal protein amount obtained using the PBC membrane were significantly higher compared to the amounts obtained by commercially available ExoQuick-TC (Fig. 5A and B).

In addition to the higher exosome yield, the PBC membrane operated at 37°C also showed the highest purity (total number of exosomes (or particles) divided by the μg of free protein in the suspension) (Fig. 5C), meaning that the PBC polymer at 37°C was able to reject free proteins or other biomolecules coming from the cell culture media more effectively than the membrane operated at 4°C and ExoQuick-TC (Fig. 4C). The efficient rejection of free proteins and biomolecules in the cell culture media by the PBC membrane at 37°C was due to the collapsed dense compact conformation of PBC layer creating strong steric and hydration barrier, significantly reducing protein adsorption and penetration [42].

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Additional comparison for the exosome isolation performance of the membrane with other established exosome isolation methods was also conducted. It was observed that the yield and purity of the exosomes obtained by PBC membrane were significantly higher than the ultracentrifugation (UC – yield: ~2x10^11 particles/mL and purity: ~2x10^9 particles/μg protein) and size exclusion chromatography (SEC – yield: ~1.75x10^11 particles/mL and purity: ~1.75x10^9 particles/μg protein) (Supporting Information, Fig. S9A and B).

Reusability of the membrane: The membrane reusability performance was also evaluated upon 5 cycles of subsequent cell culture media filtration and backwash. Considering the PBC membrane performance reported above as well as the envisioned application at in vitro cell culture conditions, the reusability tests were only conducted at 37°C and pH 7.4. It was observed that after 5 cycles followed by a backwash, the membrane kept its cell culture media permeability same (Fig. 6A). The exosome yield (Fig. 6B), protein rejection (Fig. 6C) and exosome purity (Fig. 6D) also did not change after 5 cycles of filtration and backwash compared to the fresh membrane at cycle 1. The SEM image in Supporting Information, Fig. S10 also shows that there was not significant fouling on the membrane surface after multiple cycles and backwash.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
Characterization of exosomes: The isolated exosomes were also characterized in terms of size, distribution, concentration, shape, and exosomal marker expression. The size distribution and total exosome concentration isolated using the PBC membrane and ExoQuick-TC were assessed using nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA) (Fig. 7A). It was observed that the size of the exosomes along with the size distribution obtained after isolation using PBC membrane at 4°C were relatively larger than that of 37°C and the exosomes isolated using ExoQuick-TC. The same trend was observed in TSEM images with exosomes having larger size and size distribution after isolation using PBC membrane at 4°C (Fig. 7B). This trend is expected as it is known that size and size distribution of exosomes may change depending on the temperature [81–84]. It is anticipated that exosomal membrane lipid bilayers cause swelling and promote aggregation due to the reduced membrane fluidity, increased rigidity, and enhanced hydration at 4°C, while exosomal membrane maintain compact thin structures with uniform hydration layers reducing the aggregation and improving the uniform distribution at 37°C [81–83].

We also noted that the measured exosome concentrations by NTA (Fig. 7A) was lower than that of the EXOCET exosome quantitation kit (Fig. 5A).

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
The same trend was also observed in other alternative isolation methods (UC and SEC) we used for comparison (Supporting Information, Fig. 9A–D). Certain variations in the number of detected exosomes (or particles) in different methods were also reported before [85–88]. This is due to the different detection principles of two methods, hence their output is mostly for complementary assessment rather than direct comparison. EXOCET measures Acetyl-CoA Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity, an enzyme shown to be highly enriched in exosomes, while NTA counts particles based on Brownian motion. Despite the difference in detected exosome concentrations, both methods showed a similar pattern for the yield and purity of exosomes isolated by the PBC membrane (Fig. 5) being higher than the exosome concentration obtained by using ExoQuick-TC (Fig. 7A) as well as the exosomes isolated by UC and SEC methods (Supporting Information, Fig. 9A–D).

In addition to size, distribution and concentration, the shape and integrity of the isolated exosomes are also critical, particularly for the exosomes isolated using PBC membrane at high pressures. The applied pressure and membrane pore structure could result in disrupting the exosomal membrane causing exosomal protein leaching or significantly altering the shape of the isolated exosomes. However, we observed that the isolated exosomes using the PBC membrane were found to be intact with expected shape and without any damage (Fig. 7B).

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content:
This was further supported by the exosomal membrane and exosomal protein labelling of the isolated exosomes (Supporting Information, Fig. S11). Additionally, the isolated exosomes, regardless the used isolation method or operation condition, demonstrated the expression of exosomal membrane markers, CD9, CD63 and CD81, supporting our findings for the successful isolation (Fig. 7C). It should also be noted here that although it gives a general idea, the total number of CD9, CD63 and CD81 positive exosomes (or particles) for PBC membranes (at 4 and 37°C) and ExoQuick-TC reported in Fig. 7C do not give an accurate estimate about the overall number of exosomes (or particles) isolated since one single exosome could be positive for multiple exosomal membrane markers.

Additionally, the Western blot analysis was also conducted to demonstrate that the isolated exosomes are MSCs-derived as they express MSCs associated markers, such as CD29, CD44 and CD73 (Supporting Information, Fig. S12).

The biological activity of the exosomes was also evaluated after isolation using the PBC membrane on 3T3 fibroblast cells for scratch test and on PC12-TrKB cells for neurite extension test (Fig. 8A and B). It was observed that both exosomes isolated at 4 and 37°C using the PBC membrane were able to enhance gap closure on 3T3 cells upon the scratch (Fig. 8A) and improved the neurite extension on PC12-TrkB cells (Fig. 8B) significantly compared to cell only control groups with similar efficiency.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes

content: The representative images showing the gap closure and neurite extension can be seen in Supporting Information, Fig. S13. These results demonstrated that the exosomes kept their biological activity during and after isolation.

citation.title: Stimuli responsive polymer-based membrane for isolation of exosomes
